name: क्षेत्र-क्षेत्रज्ञविभागयोग
teaching: The body, the mind, desire, aversion, intelligence — all of it is the field. You are the knower of the field. Liberation begins with this clean distinction: whatever can be watched is not the watcher. The same light knows every field, undivided, like one sun illumining the whole world. One cut creates the chapter: the field, and its knower. Everything observable — body, mind, even feeling — is field; the seer is never the seen. Whoever holds that distinction, in any life, holds the door.
questions: List what you call 'me' — body, moods, history, opinions. Which of these can you observe? What is left?
Krishna defines knowledge as humility, harmlessness, steadiness. Where has your knowledge made you less humble?
Can you spend one difficult moment today as the field's witness rather than its owner?

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव क्षेत्रं क्षेत्रज्ञमेव च ।
एतद्वेदितुमिच्छामि ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं च केशव॥
verses.transliteration:
prakṛtiṃ puruṣaṃ caiva kṣetraṃ kṣetrajñameva ca
etadveditumicchāmi jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ ca keśava
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: prakriti and purusha, the field and the knower of the field, knowledge and the object of knowledge — these I wish to understand, Keshava.
verses.translation: I wish to learn about Nature (matter) and the Spirit (soul), the field and the knower of the field, knowledge and that which ought to be known, O Kesava.

anvaya.meaning: and knowledge and the knowable, O Keshava

verses.explanation: Six technical terms in one breath: Arjuna requests the conceptual machinery beneath everything he has heard — matter and spirit, the field and its witness, knowing and the known. The Gita's final movement begins here: after love's chapters, the anatomy lesson; after the vision, the vocabulary to hold it.
verses.connection: Opens Part Three of the Gita: the analytic chapters that give bhakti its metaphysical skeleton.
verses.reflection: Ask for the map beneath your experience today: what is the field here, and who is knowing it?
verses.background: A new movement of the Gita begins — from devotion to discrimination. Arjuna asks for the chapter's whole vocabulary: prakriti and purusha, the field and the knower of the field, knowledge and that which is to be known — I wish to learn these, O Keshava.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
इदं शरीरं कौन्तेय क्षेत्रमित्यभिधीयते ।
एतद्यो वेत्ति तं प्राहुः क्षेत्रज्ञ इति तद्विदः॥
verses.transliteration:
idaṃ śarīraṃ kaunteya kṣetramityabhidhīyate
etadyo vetti taṃ prāhuḥ kṣetrajña iti tadvidaḥ
verses.essence: The Blessed Lord says: this body, Kaunteya, is called the field; the one who knows it — him the knowers of truth call the knower of the field.
verses.translation: This body, O Arjuna, is called the field; he who knows it is called the knower of the field, by those who know of them.

anvaya.meaning: the one who knows this — him they call

verses.explanation: The founding distinction, drawn in a farmer's metaphor: kṣetra, the field — this body with all its weather, crops and seasons — and kṣetrajña, the one who *knows* it. The move is small and seismic: whatever you can observe about yourself, you are not it; you are the observing. The rest of the chapter unpacks these two words. Idam shariram kaunteya kshetram — this body is the field: the verse on which Shankara mounts his most celebrated argument, the adhyasa discussion — all bondage as the superimposition of seer on seen. The farmer's metaphor is read forward too: a kshetra is sown and harvested; what is planted in this field across a life will come up. The knower watches; the field grows.
verses.connection: The chapter's axiom: everything else (13.3–35) is commentary on this one cut.
verses.reflection: Run the founding experiment once: whatever you can observe in yourself, note — observed, therefore not me.
verses.background: Krishna lays down the master distinction: this body is the field; the one who knows it is the knower of the field — and know Me as the knower in all fields. He then inventories the field exhaustively: elements, ego, intellect, senses, their objects, desire and aversion, pleasure and pain — the whole apparatus of experience.

verses.devanagari:
क्षेत्रज्ञं चापि मां विद्धि सर्वक्षेत्रेषु भारत ।
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोर्ज्ञानं यत्तज्ज्ञानं मतं मम॥
verses.transliteration:
kṣetrajñaṃ cāpi māṃ viddhi sarvakṣetreṣu bhārata
kṣetrakṣetrajñayorjñānaṃ yattajjñānaṃ mataṃ mama
verses.essence: And know me also as the knower of the field in all fields, Bharata. The knowledge of field and field-knower — that, in my view, is true knowledge.
verses.translation: Do thou also know Me as the knower of the field in all fields, O Arjuna. Knowledge of both the field and the knower of the field is considered by Me to be ï1the ï1 knowledge.

anvaya.meaning: and know me also as the field-knower
the knowledge of field and field-knower
that is knowledge — such is my view

verses.explanation: The verse that detonates the tidy dualism a breath after it was drawn: the knower in *all* fields is — me. Behind every pair of eyes, one Witness; the kṣetrajña in Arjuna and in Krishna and in the soldier across the field is not many but one, and that one is speaking. And the definition narrows magnificently: of all that can be learned, only *this* — field and knower told apart — earns the name knowledge. Kshetrajnam chapi mam viddhi — know Me as the knower in all fields: the single line over which the Vedanta schools fought for a millennium. Shankara: one witness, plurality illusory. Ramanuja: many real souls, one inner Lord ensouling them. Madhva: knower and Lord eternally distinct, the verse teaching pervasion. The fault line of an entire civilization's philosophy runs through eight words here.
verses.connection: Raises 13.2's psychology into theology: the private witness identified as the universal one.
verses.schools_note: The great fault line of Vedanta runs through this verse. Shankara reads it as flat identity — the one Self appearing as many knowers, difference being illusion. Ramanuja reads the souls as real and distinct, with Krishna their inner Self as a soul is to its body. Madhva keeps knower and Lord eternally different, the verse stating pervasion, not identity.
verses.reflection: Greet the knower behind one other pair of eyes today as the same knower behind yours.
verses.background: Krishna lays down the master distinction: this body is the field; the one who knows it is the knower of the field — and know Me as the knower in all fields. He then inventories the field exhaustively: elements, ego, intellect, senses, their objects, desire and aversion, pleasure and pain — the whole apparatus of experience.

verses.devanagari:
तत्क्षेत्रं यच्च यादृक्च यद्विकारि यतश्च यत् ।
स च यो यत्प्रभावश्च तत्समासेन मे शृणु॥
verses.transliteration:
tatkṣetraṃ yacca yādṛkca yadvikāri yataśca yat
sa ca yo yatprabhāvaśca tatsamāsena me śṛṇu
verses.essence: What that field is, of what kind, what its modifications, whence it arises and what it is; and who he is and what his powers — hear that from me in brief.
verses.translation: What the field is and of what nature, what are its modifications and whence it is and also who He is and what His powers are hear all that from Me in brief.

anvaya.word: तत् क्षेत्रं यत् च यादृक् च
anvaya.meaning: that field — what it is, and of what kind
what its changes, whence, and what
and who he is, and what his powers
that, in brief, hear from me

verses.explanation: The syllabus: five questions about the field, two about its knower — a complete research program announced in two lines. Samāsena, in brief: the Gita is about to compress all of Sankhya's category-science into a handful of verses, then point beyond what Sankhya ever mapped.
verses.connection: Table of contents for 13.6–19: the field analyzed, then the knower's true nature.
verses.reflection: Approach yourself like a researcher today: what is this field, whence, with what changes, and whose?
verses.background: Krishna lays down the master distinction: this body is the field; the one who knows it is the knower of the field — and know Me as the knower in all fields. He then inventories the field exhaustively: elements, ego, intellect, senses, their objects, desire and aversion, pleasure and pain — the whole apparatus of experience.

verses.devanagari:
ऋषिभिर्बहुधा गीतं छन्दोभिर्विविधैः पृथक् ।
ब्रह्मसूत्रपदैश्चैव हेतुमद्भिर्विनिश्चितैः॥
verses.transliteration:
ṛṣibhirbahudhā gītaṃ chandobhirvividhaiḥ pṛthak
brahmasūtrapadaiścaiva hetumadbhirviniścitaiḥ
verses.essence: It has been sung by the seers in many ways, in various hymns distinctly — and in the words of the Brahma-sutras, reasoned and conclusive.
verses.translation: Sages have sung in many ways, in various distinctive chants and also in the suggestive words indicative of the Absolute, full of reasoning and decisive.

anvaya.meaning: sung by the rishis in many ways
and by the words of the Brahma-sutras

verses.explanation: A bibliography, and a humility rare in revelation: this teaching is not novel — the seers sang it, the hymns scattered it, and the sutra-style aphorisms argued it with hetumat, *reasons*. The Gita places itself inside a tradition of both poetry and logic, claiming to distill rather than invent.
verses.connection: Credentials the analysis to come: ancient, plural, and argued — not asserted.
verses.reflection: Lean on the lineage when your own seeing tires — seers sang this, sutras argued it, before you.
verses.background: Krishna lays down the master distinction: this body is the field; the one who knows it is the knower of the field — and know Me as the knower in all fields. He then inventories the field exhaustively: elements, ego, intellect, senses, their objects, desire and aversion, pleasure and pain — the whole apparatus of experience.

verses.devanagari:
महाभूतान्यहंकारो बुद्धिरव्यक्तमेव च ।
इन्द्रियाणि दशैकं च पञ्च चेन्द्रियगोचराः॥
verses.transliteration:
mahābhūtānyahaṃkāro buddhiravyaktameva ca
indriyāṇi daśaikaṃ ca pañca cendriyagocarāḥ
verses.essence: The great elements, the ego, the understanding, and the unmanifest; the ten senses and the one mind, and the five pastures of the senses —
verses.translation: The great elements, egoism, intellect, and also the Unmanifested Nature, the ten senses and one (mind), and the five objects of the senses.

anvaya.meaning: the senses — ten — and the one (mind)

verses.explanation: The field surveyed with Sankhya's instruments: twenty-four items in two verses. Earth to ether, the I-maker, the intellect, the unmanifest seed-state of matter; ten senses of knowing and doing, the mind that coordinates them, and the five 'pastures' — go-cara, where the sense-cattle graze. Everything objectifiable is going on this list.
verses.connection: Begins the field-inventory of 13.4's first question — completed by 13.7.
verses.reflection: Inventory your equipment without flinching today: elements, senses, mind, ego — all of it, field.
verses.background: Krishna lays down the master distinction: this body is the field; the one who knows it is the knower of the field — and know Me as the knower in all fields. He then inventories the field exhaustively: elements, ego, intellect, senses, their objects, desire and aversion, pleasure and pain — the whole apparatus of experience.

verses.devanagari:
इच्छा द्वेषः सुखं दुःखं संघातश्चेतना धृतिः ।
एतत्क्षेत्रं समासेन सविकारमुदाहृतम्॥
verses.transliteration:
icchā dveṣaḥ sukhaṃ duḥkhaṃ saṃghātaścetanā dhṛtiḥ
etatkṣetraṃ samāsena savikāramudāhṛtam
verses.essence: — desire, aversion, pleasure, pain, the bodily aggregate, awareness, steadfastness: this is the field, briefly described with its modifications.
verses.translation: Desire, hatred, pleasure, pain, the aggregate (the body), intelligence, fortitude the field has thus been briefly described with its modifications.

anvaya.meaning: this is the field, in brief

verses.explanation: The inventory's quiet shock: desire, aversion, pleasure, pain — even cetanā, the light of awareness as a *function* — all filed under field, not knower. What you want, what you flee, what you feel, even your moods of clarity: observable, therefore not you. The self just lost everything it usually calls itself, and kept only the seeing.
verses.connection: Completes the field-list — and relocates the entire inner life to the 'known' side of 13.2's line.
verses.reflection: File one strong feeling correctly today: desire, aversion, pleasure, pain — contents of the field, not the seer.
verses.background: Krishna lays down the master distinction: this body is the field; the one who knows it is the knower of the field — and know Me as the knower in all fields. He then inventories the field exhaustively: elements, ego, intellect, senses, their objects, desire and aversion, pleasure and pain — the whole apparatus of experience.

verses.devanagari:
अमानित्वमदम्भित्वमहिंसा क्षान्तिरार्जवम् ।
आचार्योपासनं शौचं स्थैर्यमात्मविनिग्रहः॥
verses.transliteration:
amānitvamadambhitvamahiṃsā kṣāntirārjavam
ācāryopāsanaṃ śaucaṃ sthairyamātmavinigrahaḥ
verses.essence: Humility, unpretentiousness, harmlessness, patience, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadiness, self-restraint —
verses.translation: Humility, unpretentiousness, non-injury, forgiveness, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadfastness, self-control.

anvaya.meaning: absence of pride, absence of pretense

verses.explanation: Now the third term of 13.1 — *knowledge* — and the definition startles: not propositions but properties. The list opens with amānitvam, the deflating of self-importance, and adambhitvam, the end of performance. Knowledge, in this chapter, is not what a person holds; it is what a person has become.
verses.connection: Begins the famous twenty marks of knowledge (13.8–12): epistemology as character.
verses.reflection: Practice the first marks today: one act of unpretentious humility, one harm consciously not done.
verses.background: What follows redefines knowledge itself: not information but twenty dispositions — humility, harmlessness, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadiness, dispassion toward sense-objects, absence of clinging to home and family, even-mindedness, unswerving devotion to Me, love of solitude, constancy in self-knowledge. All else, the verse ends, is ignorance.

verses.devanagari:
इन्द्रियार्थेषु वैराग्यमनहंकार एव च ।
जन्ममृत्युजराव्याधिदुःखदोषानुदर्शनम्॥
verses.transliteration:
indriyārtheṣu vairāgyamanahaṃkāra eva ca
janmamṛtyujarāvyādhiduḥkhadoṣānudarśanam
verses.essence: — dispassion toward the objects of sense, absence of ego, and keeping in sight the suffering and fault in birth, death, old age and disease —
verses.translation: Indifference to the objects of the senses and also absence of egoism; perception of (or reflection on) the evil in birth, death, old age, sickness and pain.

anvaya.meaning: of birth, death, old age, disease
the constant seeing of the pain and fault

verses.explanation: Anudarśanam — *keeping in view*: the knower does not morbidly dwell on mortality but declines to look away from it. Birth, death, age, disease held steadily in the visual field, the way a navigator holds the reef. Most delusion is maintained by selective attention; knowledge begins as the refusal to curate.
verses.connection: The marks continue inward: clear-eyed about the body's full itinerary.
verses.reflection: Keep mortality in peripheral vision today — not morbidly, just unfiltered — and watch priorities sort.
verses.background: What follows redefines knowledge itself: not information but twenty dispositions — humility, harmlessness, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadiness, dispassion toward sense-objects, absence of clinging to home and family, even-mindedness, unswerving devotion to Me, love of solitude, constancy in self-knowledge. All else, the verse ends, is ignorance.

verses.devanagari:
असक्तिरनभिष्वङ्गः पुत्रदारगृहादिषु ।
नित्यं च समचित्तत्वमिष्टानिष्टोपपत्तिषु॥
verses.transliteration:
asaktiranabhiṣvaṅgaḥ putradāragṛhādiṣu
nityaṃ ca samacittatvamiṣṭāniṣṭopapattiṣu
verses.essence: — non-attachment, absence of clinging to son, wife and home; constant even-mindedness amid wished-for and unwished-for events —
verses.translation: Non-attachment, non-identification of the Self with son, wife, home and the rest, and constant even-mindedness on the attainment of the desirable and the undesirable.

anvaya.meaning: in son, wife, home and the rest
in the arrival of the desired and undesired

verses.explanation: The list walks into the house: putra-dāra-gṛha — son, spouse, home. Not their abandonment — the word is anabhiṣvaṅga, the absence of *entanglement*, love without the white-knuckle grip that mistakes possession for devotion. The hardest mark on the list, because it must be practiced at the breakfast table.
verses.connection: The marks reach the domestic — where detachment is most easily preached and least easily lived.
verses.reflection: Love your nearest without the clutch for one evening — entanglement loosened, affection intact.
verses.background: What follows redefines knowledge itself: not information but twenty dispositions — humility, harmlessness, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadiness, dispassion toward sense-objects, absence of clinging to home and family, even-mindedness, unswerving devotion to Me, love of solitude, constancy in self-knowledge. All else, the verse ends, is ignorance.

verses.devanagari:
मयि चानन्ययोगेन भक्तिरव्यभिचारिणी ।
विविक्तदेशसेवित्वमरतिर्जनसंसदि॥
verses.transliteration:
mayi cānanyayogena bhaktiravyabhicāriṇī
viviktadeśasevitvamaratirjanasaṃsadi
verses.essence: — unswerving devotion to me with undivided yoga, resort to solitary places, distaste for crowds of people —
verses.translation: Unswerving devotion unto Me by the Yoga of non-separation, resort to solitary places, distaste for the society of men.

anvaya.meaning: and in me, with undivided yoga
absence of delight in assemblies of people

verses.explanation: In the middle of an austere Sankhya catalogue, suddenly: bhakti — unswerving, undivided, addressed to *me*. The placement is the teaching: even in the Gita's most analytic chapter, devotion is listed as a component of knowledge itself, not its rival. Solitude and crowd-weariness follow as its natural climate.
verses.connection: Devotion planted at the heart of the knowledge-list — chapter 12 echoing inside chapter 13.
verses.reflection: Give devotion its place in your knowledge today — and take ten minutes of genuine solitude.
verses.background: What follows redefines knowledge itself: not information but twenty dispositions — humility, harmlessness, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadiness, dispassion toward sense-objects, absence of clinging to home and family, even-mindedness, unswerving devotion to Me, love of solitude, constancy in self-knowledge. All else, the verse ends, is ignorance.

verses.devanagari:
अध्यात्मज्ञाननित्यत्वं तत्त्वज्ञानार्थदर्शनम् ।
एतज्ज्ञानमिति प्रोक्तमज्ञानं यदतोऽन्यथा॥
verses.transliteration:
adhyātmajñānanityatvaṃ tattvajñānārthadarśanam
etajjñānamiti proktamajñānaṃ yadato'nyathā
verses.essence: — constancy in the knowledge of the inner self, insight into the purpose of knowing the truth: this is declared to be knowledge. Ignorance is what is otherwise.
verses.translation: Constancy in Self-knowledge, perception of the end of true knowledge this is declared to be knowledge, and what is opposed to it is ignorance.

anvaya.meaning: constancy in the knowledge of the self
seeing the aim of the knowledge of truth
this is declared to be knowledge
ignorance is what is other than this

verses.explanation: The twenty marks close, and the definition is sealed both ways: etat jñānam — *this* is knowledge, this lived constellation of humility, clarity, evenness, love — and everything else, however erudite, ajñānam. A person could hold every text and miss the list; a person could hold the list and need no text. The chapter has redefined learning as transformation.
verses.connection: Seals 13.8–12 and clears the stage for the fourth term of 13.1: the thing to be known.
verses.reflection: Measure your learning tonight by the list, not the library: has knowing become character?
verses.background: What follows redefines knowledge itself: not information but twenty dispositions — humility, harmlessness, uprightness, service of the teacher, purity, steadiness, dispassion toward sense-objects, absence of clinging to home and family, even-mindedness, unswerving devotion to Me, love of solitude, constancy in self-knowledge. All else, the verse ends, is ignorance.

verses.devanagari:
ज्ञेयं यत्तत्प्रवक्ष्यामि यज्ज्ञात्वामृतमश्नुते ।
अनादिमत्परं ब्रह्म न सत्तन्नासदुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
jñeyaṃ yattatpravakṣyāmi yajjñātvāmṛtamaśnute
anādi matparaṃ brahma na sattannāsaducyate
verses.essence: I shall declare that which is to be known, knowing which one attains immortality: the beginningless supreme Brahman, said to be neither being nor non-being.
verses.translation: I will declare that which has to be known, knowing which one attains to immortality, the beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being.

anvaya.meaning: the to-be-known — that I shall declare

anvaya.word: न सत् तत् न असत् उच्यते
anvaya.meaning: it is called neither being nor non-being

verses.explanation: The jñeya, the knowable, introduced with the highest stakes — amṛtam aśnute, one *tastes* deathlessness — and immediately placed beyond the mind's most basic sorting: na sat tan nāsad ucyate, neither 'is' nor 'is not.' Not because it is vague, but because is/is-not are categories of the field, and this is what the field appears *in*.
verses.connection: Opens the Brahman-hymn (13.13–18): the knowable that breaks the knower's grammar.
verses.schools_note: 'Neither sat nor asat' echoes the Rig Veda's Nasadiya hymn and the Upanishads' neti neti. Shankara: beyond the categories because the categories are of the intellect, itself the field; Ramanuja softens — not describable as cause or effect in the ordinary way, yet a real, knowable Lord. The verse marks where every school admits its language is scaffolding.
verses.reflection: Sit one minute with what is neither 'is' nor 'is not' — the knowable that breaks the sorting.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वतः पाणिपादं तत्सर्वतोऽक्षिशिरोमुखम् ।
सर्वतः श्रुतिमल्लोके सर्वमावृत्य तिष्ठति॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvataḥ pāṇipādaṃ tatsarvato'kṣiśiromukham
sarvataḥ śrutimalloke sarvamāvṛtya tiṣṭhati
verses.essence: With hands and feet everywhere, with eyes, heads and mouths everywhere, with hearing everywhere — That stands in the world, enveloping all.
verses.translation: With hands and feet everywhere, with eyes, heads and mouths everywhere, with ears everywhere, He exists in the worlds enveloping all.

anvaya.meaning: everywhere its hands and feet — That
everywhere possessing hearing, in the world

verses.explanation: The Upanishadic image (Shvetashvatara's verse, quoted whole): every hand in the world is its hand, every eye its eye. Read it backward and it becomes practice: whatever hand you take, whatever face you meet, you are touching the jñeya. The all-faced form of chapter 11 returns — as everyday perception rather than overwhelming vision.
verses.connection: The knowable located: not elsewhere, but as the everywhere — ch11's vision in still life.
verses.reflection: See one hand at work today — anyone's — as His hand; one face as His face everywhere.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वेन्द्रियगुणाभासं सर्वेन्द्रियविवर्जितम् ।
असक्तं सर्वभृच्चैव निर्गुणं गुणभोक्तृ च॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvendriyaguṇābhāsaṃ sarvendriyavivarjitam
asaktaṃ sarvabhṛccaiva nirguṇaṃ guṇabhoktṛ ca
verses.essence: Shining through all the senses' qualities, yet free of all senses; unattached, yet the sustainer of all; without gunas, yet the experiencer of gunas —
verses.translation: Shining by the functions of all the senses, yet without the senses; unattached, yet supporting all; devoid of alities, yet their experiencer.

anvaya.meaning: shining by the qualities of all senses
guna-free, and yet enjoyer of gunas

verses.explanation: Three paradoxes in two lines, each the same shape: present in X, free of X. It lights up every sense without owning one; carries everything while holding nothing; tastes the gunas' whole play from outside their reach. Contradiction is the only honest syntax for what stands on both sides of every distinction.
verses.connection: The hymn's method declared: the knowable describable only in paired opposites.
verses.reflection: Practice a paradox today: be fully present in your senses, and quietly free of them.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
बहिरन्तश्च भूतानामचरं चरमेव च ।
सूक्ष्मत्वात्तदविज्ञेयं दूरस्थं चान्तिके च तत्॥
verses.transliteration:
bahirantaśca bhūtānāmacaraṃ carameva ca
sūkṣmatvāttadavijñeyaṃ dūrasthaṃ cāntike ca tat
verses.essence: Outside all beings and within them; unmoving and yet moving; too subtle to be known; far away, and also near —
verses.translation: Without and within (all) beings the unmoving and also the moving; because of Its subtlety, unknowable; and near and far away is That.
verses.explanation: The paradoxes turn spatial: outside and inside, far and near — dūra-stham cāntike ca, echoing the Isha Upanishad. 'Far' for the mind that seeks it as an object across some distance; 'near' — nearer than near — for the one who notices it was never absent from a single moment of seeking. The distance was the looking.
verses.connection: Continues 13.15's paradox-chain into geography: the knowable has no 'where.'
verses.reflection: Find the far-and-near once today: sought as an object, distant; noticed as the noticing, here.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
अविभक्तं च भूतेषु विभक्तमिव च स्थितम् ।
भूतभर्तृ च तज्ज्ञेयं ग्रसिष्णु प्रभविष्णु च॥
verses.transliteration:
avibhaktaṃ ca bhūteṣu vibhaktamiva ca sthitam
bhūtabhartṛ ca tajjñeyaṃ grasiṣṇu prabhaviṣṇu ca
verses.essence: Undivided, yet standing as if divided among beings; That, the knowable, is the sustainer of beings — their devourer, and their generator.
verses.translation: And undivided, yet It exists as if divided in beings; It is to be known as the supporter of being; It devours and It generates.

anvaya.meaning: the supporter of beings — That, the knowable

verses.explanation: The crucial iva — *as if* divided: one light through many windows looks like many lights; the partition belongs to the windows. Then the full job description in three participles: sustaining, devouring, generating — the mouths of chapter 11 and the seed of chapter 7, the same one knowable wearing all three offices.
verses.connection: Resolves the one-and-many problem with a single 'as if' — the chapter's metaphysical hinge.
verses.reflection: Watch many windows, one light, on your commute today — division as appearance, 'as if' divided.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
ज्योतिषामपि तज्ज्योतिस्तमसः परमुच्यते ।
ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं ज्ञानगम्यं हृदि सर्वस्य विष्ठितम्॥
verses.transliteration:
jyotiṣāmapi tajjyotistamasaḥ paramucyate
jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ jñānagamyaṃ hṛdi sarvasya viṣṭhitam
verses.essence: The light even of lights, it is called beyond darkness — knowledge, the knowable, the goal of knowledge, seated in the heart of all.
verses.translation: That, the Light of all lights, is said to be beyond darkness: knowledge, the knowable and the goal of knowledge, seated in the hearts of all.

anvaya.meaning: of lights, even, That is the light
knowledge, the knowable, the goal of knowledge
in the heart of all, established

verses.explanation: The hymn's crown: jyotiṣām api taj jyotiḥ — the light by which all lights are light, the seeing behind every seen brightness. Then a triple identity that closes the circle of 13.1: the knowledge, the known, and knowing's destination are one. And the address, after all the cosmic sweep: hṛdi sarvasya — in everyone's heart. The farthest thing, filed under the nearest location.
verses.connection: Closes the jneya-hymn where every Gita teaching closes: in the heart — and 13.19 will say whose.
verses.reflection: Turn toward the light by which all your lights are lit — once, briefly, behind closed eyes.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
इति क्षेत्रं तथा ज्ञानं ज्ञेयं चोक्तं समासतः ।
मद्भक्त एतद्विज्ञाय मद्भावायोपपद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
iti kṣetraṃ tathā jñānaṃ jñeyaṃ coktaṃ samāsataḥ
madbhakta etadvijñāya madbhāvāyopapadyate
verses.essence: Thus the field, and knowledge, and the object of knowledge have been told in brief. My devotee, understanding this, becomes fit for my state of being.
verses.translation: Thus the field, as well as knowledge and the knowable have been briefly stated. My devotee, knowing this, enters into My Being.

anvaya.meaning: and the knowable, told in brief
becomes fit for my state of being

verses.explanation: The section signs off, and the signature is devotional: not 'the philosopher' but mad-bhaktaḥ — *my devotee*, knowing this, grows into mad-bhāvāya, my way of being. The chapter's driest analysis was, all along, a love letter's enclosure: the metaphysics exists to make the union real, not the union to decorate the metaphysics.
verses.connection: Seals 13.2–18 and re-addresses it to the lover — then turns to prakriti and purusha.
verses.reflection: Let your study end as the section ends: knowledge folded into devotion, analysis into love.
verses.background: Krishna turns to the Knowable: beginningless supreme Brahman, called neither being nor non-being — hands and feet everywhere, seeming to have the senses' qualities yet free of them, outside and inside beings, far and near, undivided yet standing as if divided, the light of lights beyond darkness, seated in every heart.

verses.devanagari:
प्रकृतिं पुरुषं चैव विद्ध्यनादी उभावपि ।
विकारांश्च गुणांश्चैव विद्धि प्रकृतिसम्भवान्॥
verses.transliteration:
prakṛtiṃ puruṣaṃ caiva viddhyanādi ubhāvapi
vikārāṃśca guṇāṃścaiva viddhi prakṛtisambhavān
verses.essence: Know that prakriti and purusha are both beginningless; and know the modifications and the gunas as born of prakriti.
verses.translation: Know thou that Nature (matter) and the Spirit are both beginningless; and know also that all modifications and alities are born of Nature.

anvaya.meaning: and the modifications and the gunas

verses.explanation: The remaining pair from 13.1 taken up: matter and spirit, both anādī — neither created the other; they are co-eternal principles, older than any beginning. But everything that *changes* — every modification, every guna-weather — traces to prakriti's side alone. Eternity is shared; activity is not.
verses.connection: Opens the prakriti-purusha analysis (13.20–24): the field and knower under their classical names.
verses.reflection: Stop blaming spirit for matter's weather today — changes belong to prakriti; you watch them.
verses.background: The two beginningless principles are set side by side: prakriti as the engine of causes and effects, purusha as the experiencer of its pleasures and pains; attachment to the strands drives birth in good and evil wombs — while in this very body dwells the supreme witness, consenter, supporter, experiencer, the great Lord. Whoever knows purusha and prakriti thus is not born again.

verses.devanagari:
कार्यकारणकर्तृत्वे हेतुः प्रकृतिरुच्यते ।
पुरुषः सुखदुःखानां भोक्तृत्वे हेतुरुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
kāryakāraṇakartṛtve hetuḥ prakṛtirucyate
puruṣaḥ sukhaduḥkhānāṃ bhoktṛtve heturucyate
verses.essence: In the production of effect and instrument, prakriti is said to be the cause; in the experiencing of pleasures and pains, purusha is said to be the cause.
verses.translation: In the production of the effect and the cause, Nature (matter) is said to be the cause; in the experience of pleasure and pain, the soul is said to be the cause.

anvaya.meaning: in the agency of effect and instrument
prakriti is said to be the cause
in the experiencership, is said to be the cause

verses.explanation: The division of labor: prakriti does all the *doing* — every body built, every instrument wielded, every effect produced — while purusha contributes exactly one thing: bhoktṛtva, the capacity to *experience* it. Nature acts and feels nothing; spirit acts not and feels everything. The whole drama requires both: a play, and an audience of one.
verses.connection: The job descriptions behind 13.2's field and knower — and the seam where 13.22's trouble starts.
verses.reflection: Honor the division of labor once today: nature does all the doing; you contribute the experiencing.
verses.background: The two beginningless principles are set side by side: prakriti as the engine of causes and effects, purusha as the experiencer of its pleasures and pains; attachment to the strands drives birth in good and evil wombs — while in this very body dwells the supreme witness, consenter, supporter, experiencer, the great Lord. Whoever knows purusha and prakriti thus is not born again.

verses.devanagari:
पुरुषः प्रकृतिस्थो हि भुङ्क्ते प्रकृतिजान्गुणान् ।
कारणं गुणसङ्गोऽस्य सदसद्योनिजन्मसु॥
verses.transliteration:
puruṣaḥ prakṛtistho hi bhuṅkte prakṛtijānguṇān
kāraṇaṃ guṇasaṅgo'sya sadasadyonijanmasu
verses.essence: For purusha, seated in prakriti, experiences the gunas born of prakriti. Attachment to the gunas is the cause of its births in good and evil wombs.
verses.translation: The soul seated in Nature experiences the alities born of Nature; attachment to the alities is the cause of its birth in good and evil wombs.

anvaya.meaning: the cause — its attachment to the gunas
of its births in good and evil wombs

verses.explanation: The diagnosis of bondage in one compound: guṇa-saṅgaḥ — the audience leaning so far into the play that it forgets it has a seat. The watcher, prakriti-stha, embedded in nature, tastes her qualities and *identifies*; and that identification, nothing else, is what books the next womb. Rebirth is not a punishment; it is a season ticket the absorbed viewer keeps renewing.
verses.connection: Answers the oldest question — why is the free spirit bound? — with a single word: attachment.
verses.reflection: Catch one moment of leaning too far into the show today — and ease back into your seat.
verses.background: The two beginningless principles are set side by side: prakriti as the engine of causes and effects, purusha as the experiencer of its pleasures and pains; attachment to the strands drives birth in good and evil wombs — while in this very body dwells the supreme witness, consenter, supporter, experiencer, the great Lord. Whoever knows purusha and prakriti thus is not born again.

verses.devanagari:
उपद्रष्टानुमन्ता च भर्ता भोक्ता महेश्वरः ।
परमात्मेति चाप्युक्तो देहेऽस्मिन्पुरुषः परः॥
verses.transliteration:
upadraṣṭānumantā ca bhartā bhoktā maheśvaraḥ
paramātmeti cāpyukto dehe'sminpuruṣaḥ paraḥ
verses.essence: The witness and the consenter, the supporter, the experiencer, the great Lord — and also called the supreme Self: thus is the highest purusha in this body.
verses.translation: The Supreme Soul in this body is also called the spectator, the permitter, the supporter, the enjoyer, the great Lord and the Supreme Self.

anvaya.meaning: the supporter, the experiencer, the great Lord
and also called the supreme Self
in this body, the purusha supreme

verses.explanation: A second presence in the same body, named by five ascending titles: upadraṣṭā, the close witness who watches without flinching; anumantā, the consenter who permits without compelling; then supporter, experiencer, maheśvara — and finally paramātmā. Within the small theater of 13.22's entangled viewer sits another, untangled: the supreme Self, nearer to the soul than the soul's own story.
verses.connection: The chapter's quiet revelation: the body houses not one purusha but the Purusha — 13.3 made anatomical.
verses.schools_note: The upadrashta-anumanta verse — witness, consenter, supporter, experiencer, great Lord, supreme Self — is the proof-text for the paramatma doctrine: a presence in the body beyond the individual soul. Vishishtadvaita and Dvaita read two real residents (soul and inner Lord); Advaita reads one Self appearing as both, the 'second' being the first unrecognized. The five titles are recited in meditation across all three.
verses.reflection: Remember your closest companion today: the witness and consenter seated in this very body.
verses.background: The two beginningless principles are set side by side: prakriti as the engine of causes and effects, purusha as the experiencer of its pleasures and pains; attachment to the strands drives birth in good and evil wombs — while in this very body dwells the supreme witness, consenter, supporter, experiencer, the great Lord. Whoever knows purusha and prakriti thus is not born again.

verses.devanagari:
य एवं वेत्ति पुरुषं प्रकृतिं च गुणैः सह ।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि न स भूयोऽभिजायते॥
verses.transliteration:
ya evaṃ vetti puruṣaṃ prakṛtiṃ ca guṇaiḥ saha
sarvathā vartamāno'pi na sa bhūyo'bhijāyate
verses.essence: One who thus knows the purusha and prakriti together with the gunas — in whatever condition existing, that one is not born again.
verses.translation: He who thus knows the Spirit and Matter together with the alities, in whatever condition he may be, he is not born again.

anvaya.meaning: and prakriti together with the gunas
that one is not born again

verses.explanation: The payoff clause, with the Gita's recurring generosity: sarvathā vartamāno 'pi — *however* such a knower happens to be living. Monk or monarch, battlefield or hermitage: once the seer, the seen, and the strands are correctly told apart, the womb-booking mechanism of 13.22 has nothing left to grip. Knowing this distinction *is* the exit.
verses.connection: Seals 13.20–23: right discrimination, in any lifestyle, ends rebirth.
verses.reflection: Apply the exemption clause to yourself: however you are living, right discrimination still frees.
verses.background: The two beginningless principles are set side by side: prakriti as the engine of causes and effects, purusha as the experiencer of its pleasures and pains; attachment to the strands drives birth in good and evil wombs — while in this very body dwells the supreme witness, consenter, supporter, experiencer, the great Lord. Whoever knows purusha and prakriti thus is not born again.

verses.devanagari:
ध्यानेनात्मनि पश्यन्ति केचिदात्मानमात्मना ।
अन्ये साङ्ख्येन योगेन कर्मयोगेन चापरे॥
verses.transliteration:
dhyānenātmani paśyanti kecidātmānamātmanā
anye sāṅkhyena yogena karmayogena cāpare
verses.essence: By meditation some behold the Self in the self by the self; others by the yoga of knowledge, and others by the yoga of action.
verses.translation: Some by meditation behold the Self in the self by the self, others by the Yoga of knowledge, and still others by the Yoga of action.

anvaya.meaning: by meditation, in the self, (some) behold
some — the Self, by the self
and others by the yoga of action

verses.explanation: Three doors to the one seeing: the meditator's stillness, the analyst's discrimination, the worker's consecrated action — chapters 6, 13, and 3 of this very book, listed as parallel entrances. The triple ātma in the first line is the destination's signature: the Self, seen in the self, by the self. Nothing external was ever required; only one of these ways of turning around.
verses.connection: Maps the Gita's own paths onto 13.24's liberating knowledge — and 13.26 adds a fourth door.
verses.reflection: Use your door today — stillness, analysis, or consecrated work — without envying the other doors.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
अन्ये त्वेवमजानन्तः श्रुत्वान्येभ्य उपासते ।
तेऽपि चातितरन्त्येव मृत्युं श्रुतिपरायणाः॥
verses.transliteration:
anye tvevamajānantaḥ śrutvānyebhya upāsate
te'pi cātitarantyeva mṛtyuṃ śrutiparāyaṇāḥ
verses.essence: Others again, not knowing thus, worship having heard from others; they too cross beyond death, devoted to what they have heard.
verses.translation: Others also, not knowing thus, worship, having heard of It from others; they, too, cross beyond death, regarding what they have heard as the Supreme refuge.

anvaya.meaning: death — devoted to what they heard

verses.explanation: The door for everyone else, and it may be the warmest verse in the chapter: those without the meditation, the philosophy, or the discipline — who simply *heard* the truth from someone and trusted it — te 'pi ca atitaranty eva, they also cross, emphatically. Śruti-parāyaṇāḥ: holding to the heard. A grandmother's faith and a scholar's realization arrive at the same far shore.
verses.connection: Completes 13.25's map with the path of trusting ears — no one is left outside the count.
verses.reflection: If all you have is trust in what you heard — use it fully today; the hearers also cross.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
यावत्सञ्जायते किञ्चित्सत्त्वं स्थावरजङ्गमम् ।
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञसंयोगात्तद्विद्धि भरतर्षभ॥
verses.transliteration:
yāvatsañjāyate kiñcitsattvaṃ sthāvarajaṅgamam
kṣetrakṣetrajñasaṃyogāttadviddhi bharatarṣabha
verses.essence: Whatever being is born, moving or unmoving — know it, best of Bharatas, to be from the union of field and field-knower.
verses.translation: Wherever a being is born, whether unmoving or moving, know thou, O best of the Bharatas (Arjuna), that it is from the union between the field and its knower.

anvaya.meaning: whatever is born, anything at all
from the union of field and field-knower
know that, O bull of the Bharatas

verses.explanation: A universal birth-formula: every existent thing — blade of grass, tiger, god — is one event happening twice over: matter shaped, spirit witnessing, joined. Saṁyogāt, from their conjunction: the world is not matter alone (which would be dark) nor spirit alone (which would be empty), but their continuous wedding. Creation is this marriage, renewed in every sprout.
verses.connection: Generalizes the body-analysis of 13.2 to all of existence — preparing the equal vision of 13.28.
verses.reflection: See one birth or beginning today as the meeting of two: matter shaped, spirit witnessing.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
समं सर्वेषु भूतेषु तिष्ठन्तं परमेश्वरम् ।
विनश्यत्स्वविनश्यन्तं यः पश्यति स पश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
samaṃ sarveṣu bhūteṣu tiṣṭhantaṃ parameśvaram
vinaśyatsvavinaśyantaṃ yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati
verses.essence: One who sees the supreme Lord standing equally in all beings — the undying within the dying — that one truly sees.
verses.translation: He sees, who sees the Supreme Lord, existing eally in all beings, the unperishing within the perishing.
verses.explanation: The chapter's crowning verse, sealed with the Gita's favorite construction: yaḥ paśyati sa paśyati — whoever sees this, *sees*; all other seeing is preliminary. The content of true sight: the same Lord, samam, in every being without exception, the avinaśyat standing calm inside everything that is visibly dying. To look at any perishing creature and perceive the imperishable in it — that is the eye this whole chapter has been building. Samam sarveshu bhuteshu tishthantam parameshvaram — the equal-vision verse the tradition treats as the chapter's diploma: who sees the supreme Lord standing alike in all beings, the undying within the dying — yah pashyati sa pashyati, that one sees. The commentaries draw the ethical corollary in the next verse and the contemplative one here: every encounter becomes darshan once the eye is trained.
verses.connection: The vision-test of the chapter: 13.27's universal union perceived as the one Lord in all.
verses.reflection: Take the seeing-test once: find the undying in something visibly aging — a parent, a tree, yourself.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
समं पश्यन्हि सर्वत्र समवस्थितमीश्वरम् ।
न हिनस्त्यात्मनात्मानं ततो याति परां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
samaṃ paśyanhi sarvatra samavasthitamīśvaram
na hinastyātmanātmānaṃ tato yāti parāṃ gatim
verses.essence: For, seeing the Lord equally present everywhere, one does not injure the Self by the self — and so reaches the supreme goal.
verses.translation: Because he who sees the same Lord eally dwelling everywhere does not destroy the Self by the self; he goes to the highest goal.

anvaya.meaning: one does not injure the Self by the self
thence one goes to the supreme goal

verses.explanation: The ethics that falls out of the vision: na hinasti ātmanā ātmānam — one stops injuring the Self by the self. Read it both ways at once: harming any being becomes self-harm, since the same Lord lives there; and ignoring one's own depth is violence too. Equal sight makes ahimsa not a rule to keep but a redundancy — whom would you hurt?
verses.connection: Converts 13.28's perception into conduct: the metaphysical ground of non-violence.
verses.reflection: Let equal vision do its quiet work today: whom could you injure, seeing the same Lord there?
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
प्रकृत्यैव च कर्माणि क्रियमाणानि सर्वशः ।
यः पश्यति तथात्मानमकर्तारं स पश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
prakṛtyaiva ca karmāṇi kriyamāṇāni sarvaśaḥ
yaḥ paśyati tathātmānamakartāraṃ sa paśyati
verses.essence: And one who sees that actions are performed everywhere by prakriti alone, and likewise that the Self is not the doer — that one truly sees.
verses.translation: He sees, who sees that all actions are performed by Nature alone and that the Self is actionless.

anvaya.meaning: who sees — and likewise the Self
as the non-doer — that one sees

verses.explanation: The second seeing-test: all activity, sarvaśaḥ, credited to nature's account; the Self standing as akartā, the non-doer — 3.27's teaching now part of liberating vision itself. Watch any action closely enough — a hand moving, a thought forming — and find machinery operating and a witness present, with no third party doing. The doer was always a misreading.
verses.connection: Pairs with 13.28 as the chapter's twin visions: the Lord in all, the Self in none of the doing.
verses.reflection: Watch one action closely today — machinery operating, witness present, no third party doing.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
यदा भूतपृथग्भावमेकस्थमनुपश्यति ।
तत एव च विस्तारं ब्रह्म सम्पद्यते तदा॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā bhūtapṛthagbhāvamekasthamanupaśyati
tata eva ca vistāraṃ brahma sampadyate tadā
verses.essence: When one perceives the separate existence of beings as resting in the One, and their expansion from That alone — then one attains Brahman.
verses.translation: When a man sees the whole variety of beings as resting in the One, and spreading forth from That alone, he then becomes Brahman.

anvaya.meaning: when the separate existence of beings
as resting in the One, one perceives
and from That alone, the expansion

verses.explanation: The vision in motion, both directions at once: all multiplicity seen folding back into one ground, and the whole spread of things seen pouring out from it — the ocean in the waves, the waves from the ocean. Brahma sampadyate: at that seeing, one does not travel to Brahman; one *amounts to* it. The arrival is the perception.
verses.connection: The unitive climax of the three visions (13.28, 13.30, 13.31): diversity read as one substance unfolding.
verses.reflection: Trace the many back to the One once today, and the One out into the many — both directions, one look.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
अनादित्वान्निर्गुणत्वात्परमात्मायमव्ययः ।
शरीरस्थोऽपि कौन्तेय न करोति न लिप्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
anāditvānnirguṇatvātparamātmāyamavyayaḥ
śarīrastho'pi kaunteya na karoti na lipyate
verses.essence: Being without beginning and without gunas, this imperishable supreme Self, though dwelling in the body, Kaunteya, neither acts nor is stained.
verses.translation: Being without beginning and being devoid of (any) alities, the Supreme Self, imperishable, though dwelling in the body, O Arjuna, neither acts nor is tainted.

anvaya.meaning: though dwelling in the body, O Kaunteya

verses.explanation: The residency clause: śarīra-stho 'pi — *even while in the body*, the supreme Self does nothing and catches nothing. The logic is given, not just asserted: having no beginning, it never entered the causal stream; having no gunas, it offers karma no surface to adhere to. Two similes now follow to make the untouchability visible.
verses.connection: States the immunity that 13.33–34 will illustrate with space and sun.
verses.reflection: Carry the immunity clause through a messy day: dwelling in it all, doing none of it, stained by none.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
यथा सर्वगतं सौक्ष्म्यादाकाशं नोपलिप्यते ।
सर्वत्रावस्थितो देहे तथात्मा नोपलिप्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā sarvagataṃ saukṣmyādākāśaṃ nopalipyate
sarvatrāvasthito dehe tathātmā nopalipyate
verses.essence: As the all-pervading space, by its subtlety, is not stained — so the Self, present everywhere in the body, is not stained.
verses.translation: As the all-pervading ether is not tainted, because of its subtlety, so the Self seated everywhere in the body is not tainted.

anvaya.meaning: so the Self is not stained

verses.explanation: The first simile: ākāśa, space — present in the sewer and the shrine equally, dirtied by neither, because its subtlety gives filth nothing to land on. So the Self in the body: host to every process, participant in none. Whatever the body and mind have done or suffered, the space they happened *in* remains exactly as clean as before any of it.
verses.connection: Illustrates 13.32's non-staining: omnipresence without contamination.
verses.reflection: Be like space in one difficult room today: fully present, hosting everything, dirtied by nothing.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
यथा प्रकाशयत्येकः कृत्स्नं लोकमिमं रविः ।
क्षेत्रं क्षेत्री तथा कृत्स्नं प्रकाशयति भारत॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā prakāśayatyekaḥ kṛtsnaṃ lokamimaṃ raviḥ
kṣetraṃ kṣetrī tathā kṛtsnaṃ prakāśayati bhārata
verses.essence: As the one sun illumines this entire world — so the owner of the field illumines the entire field, Bharata.
verses.translation: Just as the one sun illumines the whole world, so also the Lord of the field (Supreme Self) illumines the whole field, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: so the whole field, the field-owner

verses.explanation: The second simile answers the lingering 'but what does the non-doer *do*?': it shines. Ekaḥ raviḥ — one sun, the whole world lit; one kṣetrī, the entire field of body-mind made knowable. Illumination is not an action among actions; the sun ripens crops and bleaches bones without lifting a finger. Consciousness works exactly so: everything happens by its light, nothing by its hand.
verses.connection: The chapter's final image: the knower's whole relationship to the field — pure, effortless lighting.
verses.reflection: Illumine without managing once today — be the sun to a situation: light it, and let it ripen itself.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.

verses.devanagari:
क्षेत्रक्षेत्रज्ञयोरेवमन्तरं ज्ञानचक्षुषा ।
भूतप्रकृतिमोक्षं च ये विदुर्यान्ति ते परम्॥
verses.transliteration:
kṣetrakṣetrajñayorevamantaraṃ jñānacakṣuṣā
bhūtaprakṛtimokṣaṃ ca ye viduryānti te param
verses.essence: Those who, by the eye of knowledge, thus discern the distinction between field and field-knower, and the release of beings from prakriti — they go to the Supreme.
verses.translation: They who, by the eye of knowledge, perceive the distinction between the field and its knower and also the liberation from the Nature of being, go to the Supreme.

anvaya.meaning: the distinction, by the eye of knowledge
and the release of beings from prakriti
those who know — they go to the Supreme

verses.explanation: The chapter closes on its own instrument: jñāna-cakṣuṣā, the eye of knowledge — counterpart to the divine eye of 11.8, but this one is grown, not granted, through the twenty marks of 13.8–12. What it discerns is one antaram, one distinction — seer from seen — and in that discernment, the exit from prakriti's whole machinery. The Gita's most analytic chapter ends having taught a single cut, and called it the door. Kshetra-kshetrajnayor evam antaram — the chapter's final examination names one skill: the distinction, seen by jnana-chakshusha, the eye of knowledge. Commentators contrast this grown eye with the granted eye of 11.8 — vision can be gifted once, but the discriminating sight must be cultivated. Yanti te param: those who hold the one cut, between seer and seen, hold the door.
verses.connection: Seals the chapter: the field-knower distinction, truly seen, is itself the passage to the Supreme.
verses.reflection: Sharpen the one distinction before sleep: seer from seen — the single cut this chapter calls the door.
verses.background: The chapter gathers every path to the one seeing: by meditation, by Sankhya, by works, even by faithful hearing — all cross beyond death. The verses then drill the vision: the same supreme Lord standing equally in all that perishes, the Self that does not act, all beings rooted in One — like space unstained, like the sun illumining the whole field. Who sees with the eye of knowledge the difference between field and knower goes to the Supreme.
